attributes.stem:
A 59-year-old man presents to the pre-admission clinic before elective shoulder reconstruction surgery. He has a medical history of depression, anxiety, and alcohol use disorder in remission for two years. His regular medications include sertraline and thiamine. He expresses concern about post-operative pain management and potential risk of developing opioid dependence given his history of substance use disorder. He does not take any regular analgesics.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. His shoulder examination reveals reduced range of movement consistent with his surgical diagnosis.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan for post-operative pain management?
options: Interscalene nerve block with multimodal analgesia
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with valid concerns about post-operative pain management given his history of substance use disorder. The challenge is to provide adequate pain relief while minimising the risk of developing new substance dependence.

Interscalene nerve block with multimodal analgesia is the most appropriate option. Regional anaesthesia provides excellent post-operative analgesia for shoulder surgery while significantly reducing opioid requirements, and interscalene nerve block has significant evidence for this indication. The multimodal approach would include regular paracetamol, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (if not contraindicated), and possibly pregabalin, with oral opioids only as rescue medication.

Patient-controlled analgesia with morphine alone would expose the patient to higher doses of opioids than necessary and may increase the risk of developing opioid dependence. It also fails to take advantage of other available analgesic modalities. Patient controlled analgesia has excellent evidence in improving pain control, but less robust evidence in improved recovery time.

Oral oxycodone alone would be insufficient for immediate post-operative pain control and represents a high-risk strategy given the patient's history of substance use disorder. Monotherapy with opioids is generally not recommended for post-operative pain, and a multi-modal approach should be utilised.

Oral paracetamol and ibuprofen only would likely provide insufficient analgesia for major shoulder surgery. While these medications are important components of multimodal analgesia, they are inadequate as sole therapy for this procedure.

Ketamine infusion alone would not provide adequate analgesia and lacks the benefits of a multimodal approach. While ketamine can be useful as part of multimodal analgesia, it is not appropriate as monotherapy for post-operative pain management.

The key principles in this case are using regional anaesthesia where possible, implementing multimodal analgesia to minimise opioid requirements, and having a clear plan for post-operative pain management that the patient understands and agrees with. Close monitoring and early review of analgesic requirements are essential.
learningPoints: Multimodal analgesia including regional anaesthesia reduces post-operative opioid requirements.
Pre-operative planning of pain management is essential in patients with substance use disorders.
Regional anaesthesia techniques are preferred for shoulder surgery to minimise opioid requirements.
references: Hurley ET, Maye AB, Thompson K, et al. Pain Control After Shoulder Arthroscopy: A Systematic Review of Randomized Controlled Trials With a Network Meta-analysis. The American Journal of Sports Medicine. 2021;49(8):2262-2271. doi:10.1177/0363546520971757. Available online at ; https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/0363546520971757

C Small, H Laycock, Acute postoperative pain management, British Journal of Surgery, Volume 107, Issue 2, January 2020, Pages e70–e80, https://doi.org/10.1002/bjs.11477. Available online at ; https://academic.oup.com/bjs/article/107/2/e70/6120927?login=false#375738144

Paul RW, Szukics PF, Brutico J, Tjoumakaris FP, Freedman KB. Postoperative multimodal pain management and opioid consumption in arthroscopy clinical trials: a systematic review. Arthroscopy, Sports Medicine, and Rehabilitation. 2022 Apr 1;4(2):e721-46. Available online at ; https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666061X21001759#bib16
